metadata.ogTitle: Why bitcoin keeps moving closer to financial infrastructure
metadata.twitter:title: Why bitcoin keeps moving closer to financial infrastructure
metadata.twitter:creator: @venturebeat
metadata.title: Why bitcoin keeps moving closer to financial infrastructure | VentureBeat
metadata.og:title: Why bitcoin keeps moving closer to financial infrastructure

markdown:
Contributor Content

[Jon Stojan](https://venturebeat.com/author/jon-stojan)
March 27, 2026


![image1](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F4SJ1fl60TOjJOGlRt0Ywd3%2Fc9da6dfa14354d25b23ad343b4cdadef%2Fimage1.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=85)

[Add to Google Preferred Source](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=venturebeat.com "Add to Google Preferred Source")

Bitcoin still gets discussed in volatile terms. One day, the conversation is all about price and momentum. The next day, it swings toward regulation, custody, treasury strategy, or payment rails. That shift says something important about where the subject has gone.

Bitcoin now gets pulled into conversations about infrastructure, resilience, settlement, trust, and how digital systems may fit alongside older financial ones. Even the mechanics of a [Bitcoin purchase on Kraken](https://www.kraken.com/learn/buy-bitcoin-btc) can end up leading to bigger questions around custody, exchange design, and what people are actually participating in when they enter the market.

## Why the conversation changed in the first place

Early bitcoin coverage hinged on price rallies grabbing attention, with crashes gaining more. The whole subject often got compressed into a story about volatility. That part never disappeared. Bitcoin still trades in a market shaped by sentiment, liquidity, macro headlines, and the kind of rapid repricing that can make a calm person stare at a screen longer than they meant to.

But older framing leaves out a lot of what’s changed. Bitcoin is also discussed by institutions, businesses, treasury teams, long-term allocators, and technology operators who are less interested in a quick narrative than a system design. In that context, the asset is examined for how it behaves inside a broader digital economy: the way it’s stored, how it moves, who intermediates access, and how it’s governed by code. One of the biggest components, though, is how much trust the surrounding infrastructure can actually support.

## When custody and trust become central

One of the fastest ways to understand bitcoin’s evolution is to stop with the asset itself and look at the layers around it. A digital asset can be interesting in theory and still remain marginal if the custody question is weak, the trading venue feels fragile, or the process of entering and exiting the market feels opaque. That has pushed exchanges, custodians, and infrastructure providers into a much bigger role than the early narrative allowed.

Trust in this space rarely comes from one feature. It usually builds through a number of decisions. Those include account security, withdrawal processes, and transparency around fees. Support during periods of stress is also important, as is the quality of educational material built around the product.

Exchanges now carry a lot more weight than ‘a place to buy and sell’ suggests. They sit at the point where abstract interest turns into action, and small failures at that layer can reshape how the entire market feels.

Kraken is one of the firms often associated with that infrastructure side of the conversation, especially where onboarding, platform trust, and transaction mechanics meet.

## Why institutions keep showing up in the discussion

Institutional participation gets flattened in a lot of public commentary. It’s often treated like a vote of confidence, as if one category of market participation could settle the argument for everyone else. Institutional interest may signal seriousness, but it also reflects a different kind of market behavior.

Large participants think about allocation, risk models, custody controls, reporting structures, internal approvals, and macro exposure in ways that retail narratives usually ignore. That’s important because bitcoin starts to look different through that lens.

A high-volatility asset may still be considered worth studying if it behaves differently from other holdings, if it fits into a wider thesis around monetary uncertainty, or if the infrastructure around it has matured enough to make access more workable. The move from fringe curiosity to strategic consideration happens because more types of decision-makers begin treating the subject as real enough to evaluate seriously.

![image2](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F5NE2X3xqhOMheLBgX7yI3C%2F002ea5e3e02abe96116edaf2ca6deb54%2Fimage2.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=75)

## Why regulation keeps reshaping the edges

Regulation remains one of the most awkward but unavoidable parts of bitcoin’s path into the mainstream. Some market participants want speed and minimal friction. Others are looking for clearer rules before they commit resources or recommend exposure. Both impulses are easy to understand, and neither one resolves the tension.

What regulation often does, even when it creates uncertainty, is force market structure questions into the open. How are digital assets classified? What standard should exchanges follow? What counts as compliant market access? And finally, where do consumer protections begin and end? Those issues affect institutions and individuals alike because they shape the environment surrounding participation.

Bitcoin’s future will likely continue getting influenced by legal definitions, disclosure expectations, enforcement decisions, and the slow work of drawing clearer lines around a market that grew quickly before all rules around it fully settled.

## Why exchanges now function as more than transaction venues

The phrase ‘crypto exchange’ still undersells what the better platforms are asked to do, and users arrive needing more than execution. When they tick their boxes, they’re usually looking for a place where onboarding makes sense, wallet concepts are explained clearly enough to reduce basic errors, and where security controls feel serious. People want to make sure that the platform can remain functional even when the market is under pressure.

That’s why education content has become part of the infrastructure. Exchanges often act like the first layer of contact with that economy. They also expose users to bigger questions about wallet management, asset transfer, risk controls, and the operational habits required in decentralized systems. The platform becomes part market, gateway, and trust test.

## Why does infrastructure get more attention during market stress?

During periods of intense trading activity, infrastructure stops feeling abstract. Order flow surges, support queues grow, transaction pressure rises, and every weak point in the system becomes easier to notice. That applies to crypto markets just as much as it does with more traditional trading environments.

Bitcoin’s role in financial infrastructure is easier to understand during those moments because the asset itself and the systems around it get tested together. Liquidity, custody, exchange uptime, free transparency, and security controls are important to traders.

The market may still be responding to headlines and macro shifts, but underneath that drama is a more operational story about which systems can handle stress without becoming unstable. A financial instrument can attract attention through performance, but a market grows through reliability.

## Why long-term adoption looks more like systems

Bitcoin’s future is being shaped through accumulation: better custody, more disciplined exchange design, clearer regulatory engagement, wider institutional analysis, and a deeper understanding of what participation actually involves. Those pieces don’t always move at the same speed or point in the same direction. Still, together they suggest that the conversation is maturing.

_Investing involves risk and your investment may lose value. Past performance gives no indication of future results. These statements do not constitute and cannot replace investment advice._

* * *

_VentureBeat newsroom and editorial staff were not involved in the creation of this content._

\\
Credit: VentureBeat made with Midjourney](https://venturebeat.com/technology/apple-integrates-anthropics-claude-and-openais-codex-into-xcode-26-3-in-push)

The update is Apple's most significant embrace of AI-assisted software development since introducing intelligence features in [Xcode 26](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/xcode-release-notes/xcode-26-release-notes) last year, and arrives as " [vibe coding](https://x.com/karpathy/status/1886192184808149383?lang=en)" — the practice of delegating software creation to large language models — has become one of the most debated topics in technology.

[Michael Nuñez](https://venturebeat.com/author/michael_nunez)
February 3, 2026


[![nuneybits Vector art of bold orange gradient background giant w de5ae005-57bc-4419-99b6-35be2ec9522f](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F3YLcY3tgbv5EFx2N4c7ozJ%2F6bf2e3964aa31954df08fac31fe0ca11%2Fnuneybits_Vector_art_of_bold_orange_gradient_background_giant_w_de5ae005-57bc-4419-99b6-35be2ec9522f.webp%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)\\
\\
Credit: VentureBeat made with Midjourney](https://venturebeat.com/technology/a-european-ai-challenger-goes-after-github-copilot-mistral-launches-vibe-2-0)

The release is a pivotal moment for the Paris-based company, which is transitioning its developer tools from a free testing phase to a commercial product integrated with its paid subscription plans. The move comes just days after Mistral CEO Arthur Mensch told Bloomberg Television at the World Economic Forum in Davos that the company expects to cross [€1 billion in revenue by the end of 2026](https://www.bloomberg.com/news/videos/2026-01-22/mistral-ceo-china-behind-in-ai-is-a-fairy-tale-video) — a projection that would still leave it far behind American competitors but would cement its position as Europe's preeminent AI firm.

[Michael Nuñez](https://venturebeat.com/author/michael_nunez)
January 27, 2026


[![nuneybits Vector art of red line rising. 7091569a-956d-4255-a315-4d0377c47a16](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F7zajtt3vd53mg2vXUzTqcW%2F456bf7f157c66b9617825f5be3dc9ff9%2Fnuneybits_Vector_art_of_red_line_rising._7091569a-956d-4255-a315-4d0377c47a16.webp%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)\\
\\
Credit: VentureBeat made with Midjourney](https://venturebeat.com/technology/while-everyone-talks-about-an-ai-bubble-salesforce-quietly-added-6-000)

While Silicon Valley debates whether artificial intelligence has become an [overinflated bubble](https://www.reuters.com/business/finance/opinions-split-over-ai-bubble-after-billions-invested-2025-10-16/), Salesforce's enterprise AI platform quietly added 6,000 new customers in a single quarter — a 48% increase that executives say demonstrates a widening gap between speculative AI hype and deployed enterprise solutions generating measurable returns.

[Michael Nuñez](https://venturebeat.com/author/michael_nunez)
December 22, 2025


[![VentureBeat created with Imagen ](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2FCfRgh6sqx8GWkn45L5M5I%2F5ceff5d44c23301d4d025ea724f98bc2%2Fsoc-for-budget-center.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/software-is-40-of-security-budgets-as-cisos-shift-to-ai-defense)

"With volatility now the norm, security and risk leaders need practical guidance on managing existing spending and new budgetary necessities," states [Forrester's 2026 Budget Planning Guide](https://www.forrester.com/bold/planning-guide-2026-security-risk/), revealing a fundamental shift in how organizations allocate cybersecurity resources.

[Louis Columbus](https://venturebeat.com/author/louis-columbus)
August 29, 2025


[![](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F3PpId4EqlqGTkTRx9TfUvc%2Fb8dac789bf1d19d4a41a2fd1a55e6785%2Fu5875285552_imagine_abstract_depiction_of_a_major_corporation_e00cc4c7-e01d-4491-9a61-f0dfb16f740e_0.png%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/how-intuit-killed-the-chatbot-crutch-and-built-an-agentic-ai-playbook-you-can-copy)

In the frenzied land rush for generative AI that followed ChatGPT’s debut, the mandate from Intuit's CEO was clear: ship the company's largest, most shocking AI-driven launch by Sept. 2023.

[Matt Marshall](https://venturebeat.com/author/matt-marshall)
August 29, 2025


[![](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F5k7SJZn7SMWjP9lzTH3iSa%2F67d7010c1811b94e0efbe26fd087379b%2Fasics-advance-against-nvidia.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/nvidias-strong-q2-results-cant-mask-the-asic-challenge-in-their-future)

[Nvidia](https://www.nvidia.com/) reported $46.7 billion in revenue for fiscal Q2 2026 in their earnings announcement and call yesterday, with data center revenue hitting $41.1 billion, up 56% year over year. The company also released guidance for Q3, predicting a $54 billion quarter.

[Louis Columbus](https://venturebeat.com/author/louis-columbus)
August 28, 2025


[![](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2FuWVLq6FXHi9ytCjeZGWPL%2Fbfbf2d933af7aa3aa4a294715e694d81%2FAscend-123-Kuse.ai-hero.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/at-21-he-bootstrapped-kuse-ai-to-10m-arr-in-60-days-no-vc-zero-marketing-spend)

_No VC. No ads. Just product._

[Jon Stojan](https://venturebeat.com/author/jon-stojan-ascend)
August 26, 2025


[![Image credit: VentureBeat with ChatGPT](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F45kxiuYsYJdBSvBMQEoYkZ%2Fdd7458efc8d15e3ebbc024147890e724%2Fcomputer-use-agent.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/opencuas-open-source-computer-use-agents-rival-proprietary-models-from-openai-and-anthropic)

A new framework from researchers at [The University of Hong Kong](https://www.hku.hk/) (HKU) and collaborating institutions provides an open source foundation for creating robust AI agents that can operate computers. The framework, called [OpenCUA](https://opencua.xlang.ai/), includes the tools, data, and recipes for scaling the development of computer-use agents (CUAs).

[Ben Dickson](https://venturebeat.com/author/ben-dickson-techtalks)
August 22, 2025


[![](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2FGiXPiOJnMfjOpamsbvmvk%2Fe6c4e3c18640e509321e0a641e6a206e%2FAscend-119-Krish-Chelikavada-hero.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/ai-employees-not-apps-how-om-labs-is-building-the-future-of-work)

The enterprise software model was built on siloed apps and integrations, and is beginning to show its age. According to a recent report, the average company now [uses more than 100 SaaS applications](https://thehackernews.com/2025/03/ai-powered-saas-security-keeping-pace.html), each with its own workflows, interfaces, and maintenance needs.

[Matthew Kayser](https://venturebeat.com/author/matthew-kayser)
August 22, 2025


[![](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2FxHyKxLQdrg02j6RyB7qiM%2Faea0bc9b5938b5c7e7a720fcab9ae014%2FAscend-122-Amol-Soin-hero.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/teenage-innovators-three-brothers-are-changing-healthcare-from-their-home)

Big ideas often sprout from unexpected places like garages and dorm rooms, but three remarkable teenage brothers, Aviraj, Dhilen, and Brijen Soin, are proving that groundbreaking science and entrepreneurship don’t need a college degree. The siblings, still in high school, are the inspiration behind [Soin Pharmaceuticals](http://soinpharmaceuticals.org/) and the collaborators of several medical innovations, and their journey from a home lab to the forefront of pharmaceutical development is a one-of-a-kind story.

[K.H. Koehler](https://venturebeat.com/author/k-h-koehler)
August 22, 2025


[![Credit: VentureBeat using Claude](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F5x6jPYz36g5tOolFwdRrfG%2Fe35ed94cfee253935c30883ab1e44185%2Fhero-image-for-walmart-CISO-interview.jpg%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/four-big-enterprise-lessons-from-walmart-ai-security-agentic-risks-identity-reboot-velocity-with-governance-and-ai-vs-ai-defense)

VentureBeat recently sat down (virtually) with Jerry R. Geisler III, Executive Vice President and Chief Information Security Officer at [Walmart Inc](https://www.walmart.com/)., to gain insights into the cybersecurity challenges the world's largest retailer faces as AI becomes increasingly autonomous.

[Louis Columbus](https://venturebeat.com/author/louis-columbus)
August 21, 2025


[![](https://venturebeat.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.ctfassets.net%2Fjdtwqhzvc2n1%2F68E6oEJtNBk8naRxMTRPvd%2F75bbca1375c3011449b2612dd0d0bc08%2F0_2.png%3Fw%3D1000%26q%3D100&w=3840&q=50)](https://venturebeat.com/business/how-ai-digital-minds-startup-delphi-stopped-drowning-in-user-data-and-scaled-up-with-pinecone)

[Delphi](https://www.delphi.ai/), a two-year-old San Francisco AI startup named [after the Ancient Greek oracle](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Delphic-oracle), was facing a **thoroughly 21st-century problem: its “Digital Minds”**— interactive, personalized chatbots modeled after an end-user and meant to channel their voice based on their writings, recordings, and other media — **were drowning in data.**

[Carl Franzen](https://venturebeat.com/author/carlfranzen)
August 21, 2025
